video_title: Neural Networks and Deep Learning Fundamentals
speaker: Prof. James Carter

chapters.title: From Biological to Artificial Neurons
chapters.transcript: Welcome to today's lecture on neural networks and deep learning. I am Prof. James Carter, and today we are going to explore one of the most exciting and impactful developments in artificial intelligence. Neural networks were inspired by the structure and function of the human brain. Our brains consist of approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of other neurons through synapses. Information flows through these connections as electrical signals. When a neuron receives enough input signal, it fires and sends its own signal to connected neurons. The artificial neuron, also called a perceptron, was invented by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958, inspired by this biological model. An artificial neuron takes multiple numerical inputs, multiplies each by a weight, sums everything together, and then applies an activation function to produce an output. Mathematically, if our inputs are x1 through xn, our weights are w1 through wn, and our bias is b, then the neuron computes the sum of wi times xi plus b, and passes this through an activation function f. The weights represent the strength of each connection — a high weight means that input has a strong influence on the output. Learning in a neural network means adjusting these weights so the network produces the correct output. The bias term allows the neuron to shift its activation threshold. This is analogous to how synaptic strength in biological neurons changes with learning.
frame_captions: Diagram of biological neuron with dendrites, axon, and synapse labeled
Comparison diagram: biological neuron vs artificial neuron
Mathematical formula for artificial neuron: y = f(sum of w_i * x_i + b)
Portrait of Frank Rosenblatt with year 1958 and perceptron invention

chapters.title: Activation Functions and Their Properties
chapters.transcript: The activation function is a critical component of a neuron. Without it, neural networks would only be able to represent linear functions, no matter how many layers we stack. The activation function introduces non-linearity, which is what allows neural networks to learn complex, non-linear patterns. Let's go through the most common activation functions. The sigmoid function maps any input to a value between 0 and 1. Its formula is 1 divided by 1 plus e to the negative x. It was historically popular because its output can be interpreted as a probability. However, it suffers from the vanishing gradient problem — when the input is very large or very small, the gradient becomes nearly zero, making it very hard for the network to learn. The hyperbolic tangent function, or tanh, is similar to sigmoid but maps inputs to values between negative 1 and 1. It is zero-centered, which makes optimization somewhat easier than sigmoid. But it still suffers from vanishing gradients for large inputs. The Rectified Linear Unit, or ReLU, is currently the most widely used activation function. It is simply f of x equals maximum of 0 and x. It is computationally very efficient, does not saturate for positive values, and helps mitigate the vanishing gradient problem. However it has a problem called dying ReLU — neurons can get stuck outputting zero for all inputs if their weights become negative. Leaky ReLU, Parametric ReLU, and ELU are variants that address this. For output layers, the choice depends on the task: sigmoid for binary classification, softmax for multi-class classification where we want a probability distribution over classes, and linear (no activation) for regression.
frame_captions: Graph of sigmoid function with formula
Graph of tanh function showing zero-centered property
Graph of ReLU function: flat at zero for negatives, linear for positives
Comparison table of activation functions: formula, range, pros, cons
Softmax formula and example showing probabilities summing to 1

chapters.title: Multilayer Perceptrons and Forward Propagation
chapters.transcript: A single neuron is limited in what it can learn. A multilayer perceptron, or MLP, stacks multiple layers of neurons to create a network capable of learning highly complex functions. An MLP has three types of layers. The input layer receives the raw features — its size equals the number of input features. Hidden layers perform transformations on the data — we can have one or many hidden layers, and their size is a hyperparameter we choose. The output layer produces the final prediction. The depth of a network refers to the number of layers, and the width refers to the number of neurons in each layer. Deep neural networks are simply networks with many hidden layers. The process of computing the output from the input is called forward propagation. We compute layer by layer from left to right. For each layer, we multiply the previous layer's output by the weight matrix, add the bias vector, and apply the activation function. If we denote the output of layer l as a superscript l, then a superscript l equals f of W superscript l times a superscript l minus 1 plus b superscript l. In matrix notation this is very clean and efficient to compute. The power of MLPs comes from the Universal Approximation Theorem, which states that a neural network with a single hidden layer containing enough neurons can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary accuracy. In practice, deeper networks with fewer neurons per layer tend to generalize better than very wide shallow networks. This is one reason why deep learning — using many layers — has become the dominant approach.
frame_captions: Diagram of MLP architecture with input, two hidden layers, and output layer
Forward propagation computation shown layer by layer with matrices
Universal Approximation Theorem statement on slide
Comparison: wide shallow network vs narrow deep network

chapters.title: Loss Functions and Gradient Descent
chapters.transcript: Now that we understand how a neural network makes predictions through forward propagation, we need to understand how it learns — that is, how it adjusts its weights to improve predictions. This requires two things: a loss function to measure how wrong the predictions are, and an optimization algorithm to reduce that loss. The loss function quantifies the difference between our model's predictions and the true labels. For regression problems, the most common choice is Mean Squared Error, or MSE, which computes the average of the squared differences between predictions and targets. Squaring penalizes large errors more heavily and ensures the loss is always positive. For binary classification, we use Binary Cross-Entropy loss, also called log loss. This measures the difference between the predicted probability and the true label in a way that is well-suited to probability outputs. For multi-class classification, we use Categorical Cross-Entropy. The goal of training is to find the weights that minimize the loss function. This is an optimization problem. Gradient descent is the primary algorithm used. The key insight is that the gradient of the loss with respect to the weights tells us the direction of steepest increase. So if we move the weights in the opposite direction of the gradient, we move toward lower loss. The update rule is: new weights equals old weights minus learning rate times gradient. The learning rate, often called alpha or eta, controls how large each update step is. Too large a learning rate and we might overshoot the minimum and diverge. Too small and training will be very slow. Choosing the right learning rate is one of the most important hyperparameter decisions in deep learning.
frame_captions: MSE formula and visual showing squared error for each data point
Binary cross-entropy formula with graph showing loss for probabilities
3D loss landscape showing gradient descent trajectory
Learning rate comparison: too small, too large, just right — showing convergence

chapters.title: Backpropagation: How Neural Networks Learn
chapters.transcript: Backpropagation is the algorithm that makes training deep neural networks practical. It efficiently computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to every weight in the network using the chain rule of calculus. Before backpropagation was popularized in the 1980s, training neural networks with many layers was computationally infeasible. The key insight of backpropagation is that we can reuse computations through dynamic programming. We first do a forward pass to compute the output and the loss. Then we propagate the error signal backwards through the network, from the output layer back to the input layer, computing the gradient for each layer's weights along the way. The chain rule tells us that the gradient of the loss with respect to the weights in an early layer equals the product of many partial derivatives chained together. Backpropagation organizes this computation efficiently so we compute each gradient once. Let me walk through a concrete example. Suppose we have a two-layer network. In the backward pass, we first compute the gradient of the loss with respect to the output layer weights. Then we propagate the error through the output layer to get the gradient of the loss with respect to the hidden layer activations. Then we compute the gradient with respect to the hidden layer weights. Modern deep learning frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow implement automatic differentiation, which means they compute these gradients automatically — you just define the forward pass and the framework handles backpropagation for you. This is one of the most important engineering advances that has made deep learning accessible to so many practitioners.
frame_captions: Forward pass diagram with activations computed left to right
Backward pass diagram with gradients flowing right to left
Chain rule mathematical derivation on whiteboard
PyTorch code example showing loss.backward() for automatic differentiation

chapters.title: Training in Practice: Batches, Epochs, and Optimizers
chapters.transcript: Let's now talk about the practical aspects of training neural networks. In theory, gradient descent computes the gradient using all training examples — this is called batch gradient descent. In practice, this is too slow for large datasets. Instead, we use mini-batch gradient descent where we compute the gradient using a small subset of examples, called a mini-batch, typically between 32 and 512 samples. Stochastic gradient descent, or SGD, is the extreme case where the batch size is 1. Mini-batch gradient descent offers a good balance: it is computationally efficient, introduces some noise that can help escape local minima, and can be parallelized on modern hardware. One pass through the entire training dataset is called an epoch. We typically train for many epochs — anywhere from 10 to hundreds depending on the problem. We monitor the validation loss during training to decide when to stop — ideally we stop when the validation loss starts increasing even though training loss keeps decreasing, which indicates overfitting. This is called early stopping. Vanilla SGD is rarely used in modern deep learning. Instead, adaptive optimizers are preferred. Adam, which stands for Adaptive Moment Estimation, is currently the most popular. It maintains a running average of past gradients and their squared values to adapt the learning rate for each parameter. Adam is robust, requires little tuning, and works well across many different problems. Other popular optimizers include AdaGrad, RMSprop, and AdamW, which adds weight decay regularization to Adam. In practice, start with Adam, and only try others if you have a good reason.
frame_captions: Diagram comparing Batch GD vs Mini-Batch GD vs SGD with trade-offs
Training and validation loss curves showing early stopping point
Adam optimizer update equations on slide
Comparison table: SGD, AdaGrad, RMSprop, Adam — convergence speed and stability

chapters.title: Summary and Introduction to Convolutional Networks
chapters.transcript: We covered a lot today. Let's recap. We started with the biological inspiration for artificial neurons and built up to the multilayer perceptron architecture. We discussed activation functions and why non-linearity is essential. We then covered loss functions for different problem types, gradient descent for optimization, and backpropagation as the efficient algorithm for computing gradients. Finally, we discussed practical aspects: batching, epochs, early stopping, and modern optimizers like Adam. In the next lecture, we will move to Convolutional Neural Networks, or CNNs, which are specifically designed for image data. CNNs exploit the spatial structure of images using local filters that share weights across the image. This makes them dramatically more parameter-efficient than fully-connected networks for images, and they have been responsible for breakthroughs in computer vision starting with AlexNet in 2012. Please work through the backpropagation derivation on the exercise sheet before next class — it is essential that you understand this deeply, not just conceptually. For those who want to go deeper, I highly recommend Chapter 6 of the Deep Learning book by Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville, which is freely available online.
